fed_monetary: The FOMC held the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75% for the second consecutive meeting on March 18 (11-1 vote), navigating a stagflationary crosscurrent of above-target inflation and a war-driven energy price shock. NY Fed President Williams, speaking April 7, calibrates full-year headline inflation at approximately 2.75%, cautioning that WTI at $96.10 and persistent pass-through from energy into core components argue for an extended hold — markets are pricing no cut until late 2026 at the earliest. The Fed balance sheet (WALCL $6.68T) continues its measured rundown while U.S. reserve balances at $3,026.7B and near-zero RRP ($0.2B) keep front-end liquidity adequate, even as the composite liquidity score sits at only the 33rd percentile globally.
labor_data: March nonfarm payrolls surged +178K versus the +100K consensus, snapping back sharply from February's strike-distorted print and removing any near-term justification for dovish Fed re-pricing. The unemployment rate held at 4.3% (in-line), and initial claims of 219K remain non-alarming despite printing slightly above the 210K estimate, suggesting the labor market is resilient but not tightening further. The combination of a strong payroll beat and stable unemployment signals a labor market in equilibrium — supportive of consumption but insufficient to catalyze the demand-led disinflation the Fed requires before resuming its easing cycle.
fiscal_supply: Treasury projects borrowing of $683B across the January–June 2026 window, with coupon and FRN auction sizes held steady per the February quarterly refunding; the next refunding announcement is scheduled for May 4, 2026. The TGA balance of $847.7B provides a meaningful near-term buffer, but CBO's baseline shows the annual deficit trajectory remains above $1T, with net interest cost running approximately $10B per week — a persistent headwind to duration absorption. With the 10-year yield at 4.33% and the 5-year breakeven at 2.56%, real term premium is modestly positive; any upside CPI surprise from the Iran war energy shock risks steepening the curve and pressuring auction tails.
corporate: HY OAS at 294 bps and IG OAS at 105 bps indicate risk premiums are being partially restored from historically tight year-start levels, consistent with April's typical pattern of spread widening before recovery. Loomis Sayles projects low-to-mid single-digit total returns for publicly traded corporate credit through 2026, while Man Group notes all-in yields remain healthy and fundamentals do not point to an imminent default cycle absent a material growth deterioration. The VIX at 19.8 (down 5.89% on the session) and SPX at 6,830 (+0.69%) reflect a tentative risk-on tilt following ceasefire news, though the NFCI at -0.433 signals financial conditions remain modestly accommodative — a state that limits the Fed's urgency to ease.
next_catalyst: The single most important event in the next 24 hours is the BLS March CPI release at 8:30 ET on April 10, 2026. Oxford Economics forecasts headline CPI will print well above 3% year-over-year driven by a ~10.6% monthly surge in energy prices; a print at or above that threshold would materially reprice the rate-cut path and test the 4.33% 10-year yield ceiling.
sources: Fed holds rates at 3.50–3.75% for second straight meeting, cites inflation and war risks — Federal Reserve / CNBC, March 18, 2026
